To verify knapsack problem, we need to check that solution to (1) the items of solution is valid (they belong to the original set, no duplicate or invalid value) O(n), and (2) sum of weights of items is smaller than the limit O(n), (3) sum of value of chosen items is greater than k O(n). K = {(set of item with w, value, and limit weight) | (1), (2), (3) conditions} ∈ NP as (1), (2), (3) can be checked in polynomial time O([UNCERTAIN]).

Prove that this problem can be verified in polynomial time. G: graph, node a, node b. Language: A = {(G, a, b) [UNCERTAIN] | sum of all edge weights of the path (a, ..., b) is [UNCERTAIN] k}. To verify this, we need to (1) check if this is a valid path, for each edge check if the 2nd vertex is also the start vertex of the next consecutive edge. (2) iterate through all edge weight to sum up total cost O(n) (then compare with [UNCERTAIN]). => A ∈ NP.

Construct a graph G' so that if G' has a Tonian path, by adding dummy vertices to G'(add m dummy nodes], we can always construct a graph G' from G and the TONIAN problem in G' is solved with the solution a -> b -> c -> d [4 out of 8 nodes]. Then Hamiltonian reduced to tonian, a -> b -> ... -> d is also the solution of Hamiltonian problem. Graph construction cost O(|V| + |E|). Check if that is valid tonian. => Hamiltonian ≤p tonian.

We have 2 types of knapsack: Fractional Knapsack (pick a portion), Normal Knapsack (pick a specific object). Knapsack picks the biggest-weight object and doesn't re-visit previous [UNCERTAIN: connected/considered] options, so the number of options at everytime choosing will be smaller than the total available objects. -> This can be solved in polynomial time. 1. Certificate: the weight of closest options. 2. Verifier. Step 1: Consider all closest possible options, then compare the weights. If we can choose the biggest value, continue to move to other connected options. Step 2: move till there is no option left. Return the sum.

Longest path = biggest sum of weighted edges leading to those 2 nodes. 1. Certificate: We have a list of edges with their weights (original list S). We have a list of possible edges leading to the target T ([UNCERTAIN: new/list] list L). 2. Verifier. Step 1: we pick (u,v) to check whether the 2 nodes/edges next to each other can whether exist in original list. Step 2: If step 1 return true, sum up the results. Compare results with T. Return true. Only accept when there is at least 1 true. [UNCERTAIN: Time/Complexity] can be solved just in polynomial time.

1) Certificate: Input S. Denote "0" of w as True, "1" of w as False. 2) Verifier: Step 1: check w and s have same length (for all strings s). If have same length, return True. Step 2: check [UNCERTAIN: just] position of w. If [UNCERTAIN: its] position is true, only return true if there is at least 1 true. For the true-true couple or false-false couple -> exclude that string s in S. Step 3: Move to next position of w. Continue to return true for at least 1 true in that same position of string s. Return false if there is at least 1 false in that same position of string s. Step 4: Move till the end of [UNCERTAIN: set/list] S. Only choose couples with similar results: all True (double True) or all False (double False). If the number of couples (valid) == number of strings in S, finally return True. -> This can be in polynomial time.

True. We have {a^n b^n} ∈ P. If 3SAT ≤p {a^n b^n} then 3SAT ∈ P. Because 3SAT is NP-complete, every L ∈ NP satisfy L ≤p 3SAT ∈ P so L ∈ P, therefore NP ⊆ P. We have P ⊆ NP, therefore P = NP.

False. We can only conclude that any NP problem can be solved in polynomial time (proof similar to previous question). We cannot conclude for non-NP problems. We still cannot solve halting problem, let alone in P.

We define a verifier that takes the total set of items as w and the subset of the original set and a number k as c [and the limit L]. The verifier checks if the items in the subset combined has weight ≤ L and value ≥ k. This can be done with a simple traversal to add all of them together, only at most n times (n is the number of items in the original set). This verifier is in P, so the language is in NP.

We define a verifier that takes the original graph as w and a path in the graph and a number k as c. The verifier checks if this path is valid and the total weight is > k. This can be done by a simple loop to check and add all edges connecting adjacent vertices in the path, which is at most as long as n = |V|. This verifier is in P, so the language is in NP.

Given graph G=(V,E), construct G'=(V',E'). For all v_i ∈ V, add v_i1 and v_i2 to V'. Therefore |V'|=2|V|. [UNCERTAIN: crossed-out edge-construction line]. Now, visually G' looks like G duplicated. It basically has 2 of G's as 2 forests in G'. If there exists a Tonian path in G', it must be limited to 1 forest in G'. But because it contains 1/2 |V'| = |V|, the path must be connecting every node in that forest. That is the Hamiltonian path on G (half of G'). The reduction process is only building G'. It is in polynomial time O(|V|+|E|) ∈ O(n^2). Because there exists a polynomial time reduction, we have HAMILTONIAN ≤p TONIAN.

To know whether the knapsack problem is in NP or not, we need to build a verifier on inputs of a number of items and the weight's bound. To check that in the constraint of number of items and weight bound, the maximum value from the input is the most maximized outcomes, we can use an algorithm with the time complexity O(n). Since verifying this in polynomial time, we can conclude that knapsack problem is in NP.

To know whether the problem of finding the longest path between 2 nodes in weighted graph is in NP or not, we need to show that it can be verified in polynomial time on inputs which are the path and the weight of that path. Since it takes polynomial time to verify the given inputs whether satisfying the condition or not, the problem of finding the longest path between 2 nodes in weighted graph is in NP.

To polynomial reducing the Hamiltonian problem to Tonian problem, we need to show 2 things: (1) If there is a Hamiltonian path, there is a Tonian path. Since the Hamiltonian path is the path that visits all vertices in the graph (each vertex visited once), it also means that this path has visited at least half of the vertices in the graph. Therefore, if it has the Hamiltonian path, it has the Tonian path. (2) If there is a Tonian path, there is a Tonian Hamiltonian path. Given that the Tonian path is the path that visits at least half of the vertices, [incomplete].

a^n b^n is context-free language and every context-free language is a member of class P => a^n b^n ∈ P. So if we can reduce 3SAT to a^n b^n and solve it in polynomial time, 3SAT ∈ P => P = NP (3SAT is NP-complete) => TRUE.

True. Because according to Cook-Levin Theorem, SAT is NP-complete, meaning all problems in NP can be reduced to SAT in polynomial time. So, if we can solve SAT in polynomial time, we can solve all problems in NP in polynomial time by reducing them to SAT in polynomial time.

Verifier Algorithm V (use dynamic programming): Initialize a table to store values of different set of items. Compute values of smaller set of items first then store them in the table. Use the updated values in the table to compute values for bigger set of items by adding values of new items to existing sets. Choose the highest value by scanning through the table. Running time: all steps can be done in polynomial time. => Knapsack problem can be verified in polynomial time => it is in NP. (cont): check if the highest value just chosen corresponds to the same set of items as the input A. If yes -> accept, If no -> reject. Language: L = {A | A is the most optimized available set of items given the knapsack capacity k}.

Verifier Algorithm: Language: L = {w | w is the longest path between v_i, v_j in G=(V,E)}. Choose a node among the two given nodes v_i, v_j. Compute DFS on that node until it reaches the [UNCERTAIN: furthest / last] other node. Backtracking to explore all paths leading to the other node and store result in an array of linked list. Choose the longest list in that array. Compare with the input path. If similar, accept. If not, reject.

In Knapsack problem, the searching space is exponential. For every possible solution (a 2D list of selected items) we can always verify the solution whether the solution is optimal by replacing pair of item with a better pair of allowed unchosen item [order constrains w-w(item1)+w(item2) ≤ ...] under polynomial time O(n). => Knapsack problem is in NP.

The problem of finding the longest path between two nodes in a weighted graph has a search space of exponential. But verifying a path in the weighted graph G is less than or equal to another path (a reduced problem) can be done in polynomial time (O(n)). => The problem is in NP class. P/S: we check by replacing and edge between any pairs of vertices with another edge + O(|E|).

To prove MATCH is NP-complete, we need to show that: MATCH is in NP. MATCH can be reduced from 3SAT. Proof: MATCH is in NP. By simply brute force, the TC is in O(2^n). But by a verifier V to verify every solutions of MATCH problem. Step 1: s is any possible string ∈ {0,1}^n. Step 2: For any digit i-th in s, iterate the same digit i-th for all strings in S (O(n)). If yes (same digit) remove that number out of S. Step 3: Repeat the same step 2 until we move to the last digit of s. Step 4: Return TRUE if S now is empty. False otherwise. V took O(n^2), polynomial, so MATCH is in NP. Proof MATCH can be reduced from 3SAT. Let f(w) a function such that for every w ∈ MATCH, there is a f(w) in 3SAT.
True. Just by definition, a Turing machine should be able to verify this in polynomial time.

True. If P ≠ NP, NP cannot be solved in polynomial time. False. We can conclude that 3-color problem cannot be solved in polynomial time but we cannot say that it cannot be faster than 3^n. For example, it can still possibly be solved in n! or 2^n.
False. It is not proven that 3SAT is NP-complete. True. Since 3SAT is in NP complete, and 2SAT is in P.

Given a witness (a proposed solution to the knapsack problem), we can easily verify it in polynomial time. 1. Ensure the chosen items are in the original set O(n). 2. Calculate sum of the chosen items weight O(n). 3. Check if the sum is less than a budget B O(1).

Given a path between 2 nodes in a weighted graph, we can decide if its cost is less than a budget B in polynomial time. 1. Ensure the path exists O(|V|+|E|). 2. Calculate the cost of the path O(|V|+|E|). 3. Compare cost vs budget O(1).

We can reduce any instance of HAMILTONIAN problem to TONIAN in polynomial time. As Hamiltonian results in a path P_H that contains exactly one of each vertex, P_H = {v1, v2, ..., vn}. We can create a Tonian path P_T from this path by picking a random vertex v_i (i ≤ n/2) in the first half of the path and follow the path P_H for at least n/2 more vertices. This reduction is in polynomial time O(n) (n is number of vertices |V|).

We first need to prove MATCH is in NP. We just need to loop through set S and for each position in n to verify a given solution. O(n·|S|). Since the set S is finite and all strings have the same length, time complexity to verify is O(n). Therefore MATCH is in NP. I will prove SAT 3COLOR can be reduced to MATCH in polynomial time. Each color can be an index by a symbol in the alphabet {0,1,2}.

For each item you can either pick it, or not pick it. This will create branches, which we can then use non-deterministic Turing Machine to enumerate all branches at once. Since each branch can only have up to [items] nodes, it can be solve in polynomial time using nondeterministic Turing Machine.

For each iteration from the start node, you enumerate all the way to get to the neighboring nodes. In the worst case, you have to search for all the nodes and edges, which can be done in polynomial time using nondeterministic path by assume the current path is correct and search through all the unvisited neighbor edge at once. Therefore the problem is also in NP.

Let G be the graph of Hamiltonian, we can construct the graph G' of Tonian as below. For all nodes in G, add 1 leaf to it. Pick a node, any node in G, add another leaf to it. Now G' will have 2|V|+1 vertices, so the path have to cover at least |V|+1 vertices. This path will have the form of starting from a leaf we just add, another leaf, go through all of the original graph. The solution of Hamiltonian will then be just G' removing all the leaves we added.

Match can be reduced to SAT by follow: S = {I1={i11,...,i1n}, I2...}. We trim all the position with i_c = 2 so we are left with strings. Let A = a1, a2, ... be our solution. We construct, since XOR can be construct from AND, OR, NOT. X1 = {a1 XOR i1} OR (a2 XOR i2) ... This will check if there is a string that can match I1 alone then we can do X1 AND X2 AND X3 ... AND X|S|. This will check if there is a string satisfying all the input in S at some position. More explicitly, we can construct X1 as follow, if i_j = 1, we have a_j, else we have NOT a_j. For example, we have I1 = 101. X1 = a1 OR NOT a2 OR a3. Let then I2 = 011. X2 = NOT a1 OR a2 OR a3. Then our final formula to check matching both I1 and I2 will be X = X1 AND X2. X = (a1 OR NOT a2 OR a3) AND (NOT a1 OR a2 OR a3). Does A = a1,a2,a3 exist? Solving this will solve MATCH.
True. It can be decided given enough memory and running time.

Let L = {a^n b^n | n > 0}. 3SAT is NP complete. If it is known that L is in P, then there is polynomial time reduction => 3SAT ≤p L => 3SAT in P => Every NP is in P => NP ⊆ P => P = NP.

We can verify if a given solution (a set of items) is valid: total weight is at most w and total value is less or equal in k, in O(n), which is polynomial => Knapsack is in NP.

Let the problem be equivalent to: L = {<w,k> | length of w > k} where w is a path in G(V,E). We can verify if a solution is accepted or not in O(|E|) by checking if every edge in w is in E and sum of length is ≥ k => L is in NP (O(|E|) is polynomial).

If we can solve TONIAN => There is a path with at least half of the nodes. Let it be P -> then let P' be the corresponding in tance in Hamilton. Since at least half of new graph G' is visited, every original node in G is also visited at least once (as they are duplicated). Also, due to the cycle nature due to duplication, we can always shorten P' so that every node is visited exactly once.

We can reduce SET-COVER into this problem by duplicating each set to make a pair. Cost O(n) time, where set S is set of n where n is number of set in SET-COVER. If we can solve MATCH, then we can solve SET-COVER. => MATCH is NP-complete, since SET-COVER is NP-complete.

Certificate: the optimal solution from the knapsack. Verifier: define k is the max (optimal value), iterate through the solution and do the calculation. If the calculation result = k then we accept, reject otherwise. This iteration costs O(n), so Knapsack is in NP.

Certificate: the longest path (sequence of nodes and edges) with the max weight. Verifier: define K is the sum weight of the longest path, check if the nodes in the solution list are valid, check if (v_j, v_{j+1}) exist and no cycle, check if the sum of weight equals K. If all true then accept, reject otherwise. This costs O(|V|+|E|), so finding longest path between 2 nodes in a weighted graph is in NP.

We can reduce Hamiltonian to Tonian by removing half of the nodes in H. The new graph has |V|/2 nodes. If we can solve H problem, then we can solve T problem in the new graph. If we can solve T problem, we can solve H problem in the original graph (run the algorithm with twice number of nodes).

Prove MATCH is in NP. Certificate: the w string that match the symbol in s at at least 1 position. Verifier: for each string s, compare each symbol in s with each symbol in string w, record the count. If any string's count < 1 then reject. If after checking all s strings, we detect at least 1 match in each s string, accept. This can be done in O(n), so MATCH is in NP. Prove any problem can be reduced to it. We can represent each s string and w string as Turing machines. For each s string we can form n clauses, with literals. Similar with the comparing of s_i with w. Finally, glue all by ∧ symbol: (s1 compare w) ∧ (s2 compare w) ∧ ... Each (s_i compare w) will be true if at least 1 symbol match. So the whole formula will be true if each (s_i compare w) true. We can represent this as SAT. SAT is NP-complete so MATCH is also NP-complete.
Yes, by definition of NP, decided in D-time by non-deterministic TM.
False. So P ⊆ NP ⊆ NonP.
True. Because {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0} is context-free language which is in P. Then by assumption 3SAT ≤p L => 3SAT in P => P = NP, as 3SAT is NP-complete.
True. As SAT is NP-complete, then solving SAT in polynomial time means we can solve any problem that reduces to SAT in polynomial time too.
False. Because 3-color is NP, it should be solved in polynomial time, not exponentially.
False. 3SAT ≤p 2SAT, and 2SAT is NP-complete too. We have to show other polynomial time reduction from either 3SAT or 2SAT to other NP-complete problems too.
Yes. 2SAT ≤p 3SAT. Then 2SAT can be NP-complete, so other NP problems can be reduced to 2SAT and solved in D-time.
False. If it is not in P and not NP-complete, it can be in NP \ P. So P ≠ NP.

Certificate: a set of items and its weight. Verifiers: check if the total sum of each row is max. We would use for-loop to try the sum with different cases, so the time is polynomial. Therefore knapsack ∈ NP.

Certificate: given G(V,E) and 2 nodes (u,v) with its weighted value. Verifiers: 1. From u, run DFS/BFS until reaching v, and stop. 2. Save the weighted value in a list. 3. Repeat from step 1 for u with different adjacent nodes of u. 4. Finally, compare values in the list with the given value of (u,v). Time: O(|V|+|E|), which is polynomial, so the problem is in NP.

Certificate: given a set S and string w. Verifier: for i in S, for j in S[i], if S[i][j] = w[j], set var_check = True and continue, else var_check = False. If var_check = False return False; return True. Since we just use for-loop so the time is polynomial. Therefore MATCH ∈ NP.
True. NP language is decidable in polynomial time on a non-deterministic Turing machine.
True. Because NP is the class of problems that are solvable in polynomial time, so their intersection is empty.
True. Since {a^n b^n ; n ≥ 0} ∈ P, when 3SAT ≤p {a^n b^n}, given 3SAT ∈ NP, then 3SAT ∈ P => P = NP.
True. Since SAT ∈ NP-complete, so for all y ∈ NP, y ≤p SAT. Because SAT is harder, if SAT is solvable in polynomial time, so they are.
False. The time must be O(n^3).
True. 3SAT ≤p 2SAT, so 2SAT now is harder to solve, but since it's in P, so we can solve 2SAT in P too.
False. 2SAT ≤p 3SAT, so we don't guarantee that all NP can be solved in poly time.
True. If it is not in P and it is not NP-complete, it can be in NP \ P. So P ≠ NP.

Certificate: a set of items and its weight. Verifiers: check if the total sum of each row is max. We would use for-loop to try the sum with different cases, so the time is just polynomial. Therefore knapsack ∈ NP.

Certificate: given G(V,E) and 2 nodes (u,v) with its weighted value. Verifiers: 1. From u, run DFS/BFS until reaching v, and stop. 2. Save the weighted value in a list. 3. Repeat from step 1 for u with different adjacent nodes of u. 4. Finally, compare values in the list with the given value of (u,v). Time: O(|V|+|E|), which is polynomial, so the problem is in NP.

Hamiltonian: visit each node at least once. Tonian: visit at least half of the nodes. We can reduce Hamiltonian to Tonian by removing half of the nodes in H. The new graph has |V|/2 nodes. If we can solve H problem, then we can solve T problem in the new graph. If we can solve T problem, we can solve H problem in the original graph (run the algorithm with twice number of nodes).

1. Prove MATCH ∈ NP. For each position i in n, check if number of matching in all S is > 0; if all position = 0 then [reject]. If all s ∈ S match at least 1 element, satisfiable -> verifiable in O(n). Not satisfiable if [matches] at least > 0, satisfiable -> verifiable in O(n,m). Reduce from 3SAT. Treat each element as clause (v1, v2, v3) with True or False. If all false -> no SAT.
True because of the definition. A TM halts on all inputs.
True because NP can be solved in polynomial time.
True, because 3SAT is NP-complete, so all problems in NP can be solved in polynomial time.
False, we do not know the time to reduce other problems to SAT.
True, it will take exponential time.
True: reduce all NP-complete takes O(n^3), which is polynomial, 2SAT is also in P.
False, because we are not sure about other NP problems.
False.

The language for this problem is: Is there another choice of items that has the value >= k and within weight allowed? Verifier: given a set of chosen items, (1) calculate total [value/cost] and compare to k, (2) calculate total weight of item set then compare to the allowed weight given. Each step takes linear time, which is polynomial.

Language: given a path P between 2 nodes and total cost, is there another path longer than P? Verifier: given a solution, we need to check [path validity and cost]. At bottom: Given G and P, is path P their [target] path P's total cost > [k]? Verifier: sum of weights of the path, then compare to k (O(|E|)), which is polynomial.

Let G be a graph that has 1 Hamiltonian path. Define new problem: a path that visits n-1 vertices (only 1 cannot be visited) -> remove the 2 edges connecting it to the path so it's isolated. Similarly for n-2, n-3, ..., n/2: just isolate the corresponding number of vertices, and for each number of vertices in the O(2n) for removing 2 edges. So HAMILTONIAN ≤p TONIAN.

This problem is NP-complete because the SET COVER problem can be reduced to MATCH in polynomial time, and MATCH is an NP problem. MATCH is an NP problem: given a w string claimed as a solution to S, the verifier needs to check by looping through the set S and appending all strings [matching], store the index of the string into an array [position, index value of string] O(n), and find indices such that these indices span all position and cover all indices in S. SET COVER can be reduced to MATCH in polynomial time. Search for the index with the largest length in S'. For all set that contain 0, convert to string 0---; the same for 1 and 2. Append the empty string by phi if the original subset only lies in 0,1 or 2 elements (O(n)). If MATCH accepts, then SET COVER also accepts.
